en.extract:
Townsend's solitaire (Myadestes townsendi) is a medium-sized thrush, the only solitaire native to America north of Mexico.


Range and habitat
This solitaire ranges from southern Alaska, British Columbia and Alberta to northern Zacatecas in Mexico, preferring montane woodlands. During winter, it may move in search of food to lower elevations, including the Great Plains, northern interior Mexico, and even desert oases.


Description

Townsend's solitaire reaches a length of approximately 8–9.5 inches (20–24 centimetres) and wingspan of 14.5 inches (37 cm); it weighs approximately 1.2 oz (34 g). It has a short, thick, blackish bill; a white eye ring; and gray plumage with a buffy wing stripe and edges of the flight feathers. The tail is long, tapered when shut, and edged with white. Juveniles are mottled gray and white.
It perches in a rather upright position, often high on a branch and often staying still for a long time.
Though it has some similarities to the northern mockingbird, particularly as to the tail, its dark underparts, shape, size, and behavior are not similar.
It feeds primarily on berries and insects. The solitaire is amongst the most specialized of all North American birds since its diet in winter consists almost entirely of the fleshy cones of the juniper bush, and the solitary birds form territories around productive juniper patches which they strongly defend 


Voice
Its song consists of loud, melodious flute-like rising and falling phrases with "low, husky notes interspersed but no distinct pattern" and "a slightly mechanical quality." The call is a squeaky eeek or "clear, soft whistle heeh".


Nesting
They nest on the ground in hollows under banks or crevices about roots of trees or fallen stumps, making a large, loosely constructed pile of weeds and trash, hollowed and lined with rootlets. The nest resembles that of other solitaires: a cup of fine plant material on or close to the ground. Townsend's solitaire usually lays three or four eggs that are grayish-white with brown dots or splotches concentrated at the larger end.


Etymology
The name of this bird commemorates the ornithologist John Kirk Townsend.


References

General
"Myadestes townsendi". Integrated Taxonomic Information System. Retrieved 4 February 2006.


External links

Townsend's solitaire at USGS
Townsend's solitaire photo gallery VIREO; (with picture: egg clutch)
Townsend's solitaire photo, Article Washington State

'''Townsend's solitaire''' ('''''Myadestes townsendi''''') is a medium-sized [[thrush (bird)|thrush]], the only [[solitaire (bird)|solitaire]] native to [[Americas|America]] north of [[Mexico]].

==Range and habitat==
This solitaire ranges from southern [[Alaska]], [[British Columbia]] and [[Alberta]] to northern [[Zacatecas]] in Mexico, preferring montane woodlands. During winter, it may move in search of food to lower elevations, including the [[Great Plains]], northern interior Mexico, and even desert [[oasis|oases]].<ref name=Sibley>{{cite book | author=Sibley, David | title=[[The Sibley Guide to Birds]] | publisher=Knopf | year=2000 | isbn=0-679-45122-6 | page=[https://archive.org/details/sibleyguidetobir00sibl_0/page/400 400] |author-link=David Allen Sibley }}</ref><ref name=H&W>{{cite book | last=Howell | first=Steve N. G. | author2=Webb, Sophie | title=A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America | publisher=Oxford University Press | year=1995 | isbn=0-19-854012-4 | page=[https://archive.org/details/guidetobirdsofme0000howe/page/583 583] | url=https://archive.org/details/guidetobirdsofme0000howe/page/583 }}</ref><ref name=eNature>{{cite web | title = Townsend's Solitaire ''Myadestes townsendi'' | work = eNature.com Nature Guides | year = 2005 | url = http://www.enature.com/fieldguides/detail.asp?recnum=BD0271 |access-date= 2007-08-17 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20140808042040/http://www.enature.com/fieldguides/detail.asp?recnum=BD0271 |archive-date= 2014-08-08 }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Townsends-solitaire-lake-vadnais-3.jpg|thumb|left|Townsend's solitaire feeding on juniper berries]]
[[File:Myadestes townsendi, Mt Ashland Road, Oregon 1.jpg|thumb|right|''Myadestes townsendi'', [[Ashland, Oregon]], June 2014]]
[[File:Myadestes_townsendiDF09N193CB.jpg|thumb|Townsend's solitaire by [[Louis Agassiz Fuertes]]]]
Townsend's solitaire reaches a length of approximately {{convert|8|–|9.5|in|cm|abbr=off}} and wingspan of {{convert|14.5|in|cm}}; it weighs approximately {{convert|1.2|oz|g|abbr=on}}. It has a short, thick, blackish bill; a white eye ring; and gray plumage with a [[Buffy (color)|buffy]] wing stripe and edges of the flight feathers. The tail is long, tapered when shut, and edged with white. Juveniles are mottled gray and white.<ref name=Sibley/><ref name=H&W/>

It perches in a rather upright position, often high on a branch<ref name=eNature/> and often staying still for a long time.<ref name=H&W/>

Though it has some similarities to the [[northern mockingbird]],<ref name=eNature/> particularly as to the tail,<ref name=Sibley/> its dark underparts, shape, size, and behavior are not similar.

It feeds primarily on berries and insects.<ref name=eNature/> The solitaire is amongst the most specialized of all North American birds since its diet in winter consists almost entirely of the fleshy cones of the juniper bush, and the solitary birds form territories around productive juniper patches which they strongly defend <ref>{{cite journal|author=Bock, C. |year=1982|title= Factors influencing winter distribution and abundance of Townsend's Solitaire|journal= [[The Wilson Journal of Ornithology|Wilson Bulletin]] |volume=94|pages= 297–302}}</ref>

===Voice===
Its song consists of loud, melodious flute-like rising and falling phrases<ref name=eNature/> with "low, husky notes interspersed but no distinct pattern"<ref name=Sibley/> and "a slightly mechanical quality."<ref name=H&W/> The call is a squeaky ''eeek''<ref name=eNature/> or "clear, soft whistle ''heeh''".<ref name=Sibley/>

==Nesting==
They nest on the ground in hollows under banks or crevices about roots of trees or fallen stumps, making a large, loosely constructed pile of weeds and trash, hollowed and lined with rootlets.<ref>{{Cite book |year=1914 |title=The Bird Book |author=Chester A. Reed |publisher=Doubleday, Page & Co. |location= Garden City, New York |url=https://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/30000/pg30000-images.html#Page_442 |page=442|via=[[Project Gutenberg]]}}</ref> The nest resembles that of other solitaires: a [[cup nest|cup]] of fine plant material on or close to the ground.<ref name=H&W/> Townsend's solitaire usually lays three or four eggs that are grayish-white with brown dots or splotches concentrated at the larger end.<ref name=eNature/>

==Etymology==
The name of this bird commemorates the ornithologist [[John Kirk Townsend]].
